coredata.dc:title: Self-corrected dual-optical immunosensors using carbon dots@SiO2@MnO2 improving diethyl phthalate detection accuracy
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Biru
Li, Lei
Yang, Qianqian
Zhang, Mingcui
coredata.dc:description: The traditional immunoassay is widely used for pollutant detection and bioanalysis, but there are still some challenges in ensuring its sensitivity and reliable accuracy. Dual-optical measurement can prove mutual evidence to effectively improve the accuracy of the method by self-correction, which will overcome this problem. In this study, we developed a “visualization and sensing” dual-modal immunoassay based on blue carbon dots@SiO2@MnO2 (B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2) as “color and fluorescence” immunosensors. Here, MnO2 nanosheets have the activity of simulating oxidase. 3,3′, 5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be oxidized to TMB2+ under acidic conditions and the color of the solution from colorless to yellow. On the other hand, the MnO2 nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2. After adding ascorbic acid (AA), MnO2 nanosheets were reduced to Mn2+, thereby the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 was restored. Under the optimum conditions, as the concentration of target substance (diethyl phthalate) increased from 0.05 to 100 ng/mL, the method showed a good linear relationship. The fluorescence measurement signal and the color change signal of the solution visualization support each other and give the information of the corresponding material content. The results of the dual-optical immunoassay maintain good consistency, which proves the accuracy of the developed dual-optical immunoassay for detection of diethyl phthalate is reliable. Additionally, it is demonstrated that the dual-modal method exhibits high accuracy and stability in the assays, pointing to a broad range of application prospects in pollutant analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots@SiO2@MnO2
Dual-modal
Immunosensor
Diethyl phthalate
Fluorescence immunoassay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical performance of the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-based dual-optical immunoassay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21175004 ), China; the Anhui provincial Natural Science Foundation ( 2108085MB68 ), China; and the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province ( GXXT-2020-074 ), China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterization of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites. TEM (A) and SEM (F) of SiO2 nanosphere. TEM and SEM of B-CDs@SiO2 were displayed in (B) and (G). TEM (C) and SEM (H) of MnO2 nanosheet. TEM (D) and SEM (I) were showed the morphology of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites. TEM (E) and SEM (J) of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+AA.

Characterization of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites. (A) FT-IR spectra (B) XRD spectrum and (C) UV–vis spectrum of B-CDs, B-CDs@SiO2, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2, MnO2, SiO2. (D) EDS spectrum of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites.
Characterize B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 conjugate Ab. (A) The UV–vis spectrum of Ab, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 and B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-Ab. (B) The FT-IR spectra of Ab, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 and B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-Ab.

Mechanism of dual modal immunosensor. (A) UV–Visible Absorption Spectra of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanomaterial, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+TMB and B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+TMB + H2SO4 (Photograph in left top, from left to right: B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanomaterial, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+TMB and B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+TMB + H2SO4 under visible light.) (B) Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the B-CDs@SiO2 and the absorption spectra of the MnO2 nanosheet. (C) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of B-CDs@SiO2 (green, 12.77 ns) and B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 (red, 12.66 ns). (D) UV–Visible Absorption Spectra of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanomaterial, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+AA (Photograph in right top, from left to right: B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanomaterial, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+AA under 365 nm UV light.).

Dual-optical immunoassay detection of DEP. (A) The linear detection range of absorbance immunoassay. (B) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of DEP in the fluorescent immunoassay.
(A) Synthesis of antibody labeled B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposite. (B) Schematic of dual-modal immunosensor for DEP detection.
Precision and accuracy of this dual-optical method for spiked samples (n = 3, 100-fold dilution) and GC-MS/MS for spiked samples (n = 3).
Comparison of this method with existing immunoassay for detection of DEP.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: FPIA was defined as fluorescence polarization immunoassay.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: FITC refers to the fluorescein isothiocyanate.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: TEFIA is the abbreviation of Time-resolved fluoroimmunoassay.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: BA-ELISA is the immunoassay based on the Avidin and Horseradish peroxidase labeled avidin.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: FIA is the abbreviation of fluorescence immunoassay.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: g
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: HRP refers to the horseradish peroxidase.

head.ce:title: Self-corrected dual-optical immunosensors using carbon dots@SiO2@MnO2 improving diethyl phthalate detection accuracy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Chemo Biosensing, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, 241002, Anhui, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education; Key Laboratory of Chemo Biosensing, Anhui Province; College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241002, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, 450001, Henan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu, 241002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The traditional immunoassay is widely used for pollutant detection and bioanalysis, but there are still some challenges in ensuring its sensitivity and reliable accuracy. Dual-optical measurement can prove mutual evidence to effectively improve the accuracy of the method by self-correction, which will overcome this problem. In this study, we developed a “visualization and sensing” dual-modal immunoassay based on blue carbon dots@SiO2@MnO2 (B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2) as “color and fluorescence” immunosensors. Here, MnO2 nanosheets have the activity of simulating oxidase. 3,3′, 5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) can be oxidized to TMB2+ under acidic conditions and the color of the solution from colorless to yellow. On the other hand, the MnO2 nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2. After adding ascorbic acid (AA), MnO2 nanosheets were reduced to Mn2+, thereby the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 was restored. Under the optimum conditions, as the concentration of target substance (diethyl phthalate) increased from 0.05 to 100 ng/mL, the method showed a good linear relationship. The fluorescence measurement signal and the color change signal of the solution visualization support each other and give the information of the corresponding material content. The results of the dual-optical immunoassay maintain good consistency, which proves the accuracy of the developed dual-optical immunoassay for detection of diethyl phthalate is reliable. Additionally, it is demonstrated that the dual-modal method exhibits high accuracy and stability in the assays, pointing to a broad range of application prospects in pollutant analysis.

Dual-optical immunoassay improve the accuracy of diethyl phthalate detection.
Dual-optical immunoassay improves the accuracy of diethyl phthalate detection.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Immunoassays based on antigen-antibody interactions with high specificity and high throughput, have been widely used for small molecule detection and medical diagnosis [1–3]. Several immunoassays, such as the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4,5], the immunochromatographic test [6], the fluorescence immunoassay [7,8], and the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) immunosensor [9,10] have been reported recently for the quick detection of phthalates (PAEs). However, most of the reported immunoassay methods cannot be used to detect trace PAEs in actual samples due to their low sensitivity and accuracy.

As the most common type of immunoassay, ELISA has received a great deal of attention because of its visualization [11,12]. However, the instability of enzyme activity leads to poor specificity and low sensitivity of the method, which limits its application in the detection of trace substances. A quick and effective way to increase the sensitivity of competitive ELISA is to replace the absorbance signal with the fluorescence signal due to the high signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of the fluorescence signal. Recently, fluorescence immunoassay (FIA) has been developed by employing quantum dots (QDs) and other fluorescent nanomaterials as signal probes, thus expanding the application of immunoassay in practical detection [13,14]. Carbon dots (CDs), as novel nanomaterials, have become indispensable tools in disease diagnosis, environmental and food safety monitoring due to their nondestructive, real-time detection characteristics [15,16]. However, the immunoassay of a single-signal will be affected in complex matrix samples, which may make the quantitative results unreliable [17–19]. Based on the findings, we expect that combining new carbon dots nanomaterials with ELISA which can provide a dual-modal “sensing and visualization” detection.

Carbon dots (CDs) is a kind of green, stable, and highly luminescent fluorescent material. It has simple synthesis process, good light stability, and low biological toxicity. As a new type of fluorescent probe, they have received extensive attention and are commonly used in biological imaging, immune detection, and disease treatment [20,21]. The biological functionalization of CDs remains a challenge, because of the small size of CDs and the low success rate of labeling antibody, it is difficult to identify and purify the labeled antibody after successful labeling. Finding the carrier loading CDs makes protein binding easier and can effectively amplify the fluorescence signal to improve the sensitivity. SiO2 nano-capsules are widely employed because of their superior biocompatibility and easy surface modification [22,23]. Yang et al. used silica as a drug carrier for targeted delivery of drugs [24]. Wu et al. applied two-photon silica for cellular imaging and targeted cancer therapy [25]. MnO2 nanosheets are two-dimensional materials with high catalytic activity, a large specific surface area, a wide absorption spectrum, and a specific oxidase-like activity. For example, the activity of MnO2 nanozyme was used for photodynamic therapy, and the hydrogen peroxide in marine food was detected by using MnO2 as a fluorescent absorber. More particularly, due to excellent nanozyme activity of MnO2 nanosheets, when combine with 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), TMB is oxidized to TMBox as a good colorimetric indicator [26,27]. More importantly, MnO2 nanosheets could operate as potential energy acceptors to quench various fluorescent compounds via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism [28,29]. At present, great progress has been made in the application of different properties based on MnO2 nanosheets.

In this strategy, we reported blue carbon dots@SiO2@MnO2 (B-CDs@ SiO2@MnO2) as tags for “visualization and sensing” dual-optical immunoassay to detect diethyl phthalate (DEP). As Scheme 1 shows, since MnO2 nanosheets have similar oxidase activity, they can oxidize TMB to TMB+, then add H+ to terminate the reaction to produce TMB2+, which changes the color of the solution (colorless-blue-yellow) for the visualization modal. The absorption spectra of MnO2 nanosheets and the fluorescence spectra of B-CDs@SiO2 are perfectly overlapped. The fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 was effectively quenched by MnO2 nanosheets because of IFE ("OFF"). After adding ascorbic acid (AA), MnO2 reacted with AA to produce Mn2+, and the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 was restored (“ON”, sensing modal). The absorbance and fluorescence intensity of the method gradually decreased with increasing DEP concentration. We have successfully applied this dual-modal method to the detection of trace DEP in plastics, which demonstrates that this self-corrected method has satisfactory accuracy and sensitivity for trace pollutants.

Citric acid (CA), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), triethanolamine (TEA), polyethyleneimine (PEI), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Aladdin biological technology Co. Ltd. Ethylenediamine (EDA), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), phosphoric acid dodecahydrate disodium hydrogen (Na2HPO4·12H2O), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), glacial acetic acid (HAc), sodium acetate (NaAc), and absolute ethanol were obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., China. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), 2-morpholineethanesulfonic acid (MES), and ovalbumin (OVA) were from Hefei Bomei biological technology Co. Ltd, while Tween-20 and the standard of diethyl phthalate (DEP), dibutyl phthalate (DBP), dicyclohexyl phthalate (DCHP), dioctyl phthalate (DOP) and dimethyl phthalate (DMP) were supplied by Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd, Shanghai. Anti-DEP antibodies (Ab) are prepared by our laboratory.
The preparation process of buffer PBS and washing solution PBST is shown in the supporting information.

TEM images and SEM images characterized the morphologies of nanomaterials were obtained by Hitachi HT-7700 microscopy transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi, Japan) and Hitachi 8100 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan), respectively. The structure was characterized by Fourier Infrared Spectrometer (FT-IR, IR-21, Shimadzu, Japan) and X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence spectra obtained by the fluorescence spectrometer (F-4700, Hitachi, Japan) and the fluorescence intensity and the optical density in ELISA in the 96-well polystyrene plate was detected on Synergy HT microplate reader (Bio-Tek Instruments, Inc. USA) while the UV–visible spectrophotometer (U-2900, Hitachi, Japan) was used to measure the UV–visible absorption spectra. The decay time was obtained on the FLS-1000 FL Spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, US).

The blue carbon dots (B-CDs) were prepared using a procedure modified from the previous methods [30], as the supporting information shows. The B-CDs@SiO2 was synthesized by the sol-gel method according to previously reported literature [31,32]. Briefly, dissolved 4 mL of carbon dot solution (2 mg/mL) in the mixture solution of 16 mL of secondary deionized water and 7 mL of ethanol, then 0.2 g of CTAB added into the mixture solution. As the solid was completely dissolved, 50 μL triethanolamine was slowly added to the above solution, then the reaction contains stirred at 37 °C for 2 h. Thus, B-CDs@SiO2 was formed, and the products were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, washed alternately with ethanol and water three times, and stored after drying for later use.

B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposite was fabricated as follows [33]: 40 mg B-CDs@SiO2 was added into 4 mL DI water, afterwards, an aqueous solution of KMnO4 and MES (pH 6.0, 0.1 M) were added to the above B-CDs@SiO2 solution at room temperature and ultrasound for 15 min. Then, the obtained products were at 8000 rpm for 10 min and wash alternately with water three times. Finally, the obtained solid dissolved in 20 mL DI water, then 50 μL PEI was added, and the mixture solution obtained stirring at room temperature for 6 h. After the reaction, the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 particles were finally obtained by washing, filtering and drying.

As scheme 1a shows, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-Ab immunoprobe was prepared via electrostatic interaction between B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 and Ab. In brief, 400 μL Ab was slowly added into 2 mL B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2. Then, the mixture solution was stirred for 4 h at 4 °C ice-water bath with a constantly speed. Finally, the solution after reaction was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and the precipitates were resuspended with 2.4 mL PBS (pH 7.4, 0.01 M) to obtain the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-Ab immunoprobe, which were stored at 4 °C for further use.

The microplates were coated with 100 μL 4-DEAP-OVA in carbonate buffer saline solution at 4 °C overnight, followed by washing the microplates by PBST with three times and adding the block solution (200 μL) to prevent nonspecific adsorption. Subsequently, the plate was washed thrice with PBST, then 50 μL DEP standard solution and 50 μL B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-Ab immunoprobe solution were added. After 2 h at 37 °C and the washing steps, TMB and NaAc-HAc buffer solution were added for enzymatic reaction, and then 2 M H2SO4 added to quench the catalytic reaction, and the optical density of 450 nm (OD450) was recorded with a microplate reader. And 2 M AA was added into each well to reduced manganese dioxide coated on B-CDs@SiO2 for fluorescent release. Fluorescent signals were monitored via the Synergy HT microplate reader at the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of 550 nm and 615 nm.

All the food-contacted plastics were purchased from Auchan supermarket in Wuhu, China. The DEP in food-related plastics were extract according the Chinese national standard GB 31604.30–2016. Briefly, the food contacted plastic cut into small pieces and weighted 1.0 g into 100 mL conical flask, then 40 mL n-hexane was added flowing by sonication at 40 °C for 30 min. Next, collected the extraction and extract the filter residue with n-hexane in the same conditions. After that, the extraction solution was collected together and the solvent was removed by vacuum distillation. Finally, the obtained product was constant volume to 2 mL of anhydrous ethanol and stored in volumetric flask at 4 °C. Lastly, the above samples were detected by the developed method and GC-MS/MS, and the recovery of the above samples was compared with the standard GC-MS/MS, and the t-test was used for statistical analysis.

The TEM image (Fig. S1) of B-CDs could be to be spherical shape with the uniform sizes of the well-dispersed. For a better understanding of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposite, the TEM, SEM, FT-IR, Raman, XRD, UV–vis and FL measurements were carried out. TEM and SEM images were utilized to reveal the structure and morphology of SiO2 nanoparticles, B-CDs@SiO2 nanoparticles, MnO2 nanosheets, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites and B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2+ ascorbic acid (AA). As Fig. 1 A and F show, SiO2 is present as uniformly spherical and well dispersed, with a distribution about 180 nm. Fig. 1B and G depicted typical TEM and SEM images of B-CDs@SiO2 in conjunction with B-CDs and SiO2 nanospheres. Compared with SiO2 nanosphere, B-CDs@SiO2 basically preserved as-prepared SiO2 nanoparticles have an average diameter of ∼180 nm and the solution with pale-yellow (Fig. S2). The TEM and SEM images of B-CDs@SiO2 nanoparticles further proved that the nanocrystals of SiO2 still maintained after B-CDs are embed in silica nanosphere. Furthermore, ultrathin and transparent lamellar MnO2 nanosheets with ample graphene-like wrinkles and folds have been observed with TEM (Fig. 1C) and SEM (Fig. 1H), exhibiting a typical two-dimensional morphology. The TEM (Fig. 1D) and SEM (Fig. 1I) images displayed that the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites exhibit excellent mono-dispersion with sunflower-like structure because of the ultrathin lamellar MnO2 nanosheets growth on the B-CDs@SiO2 nanoparticles. After coating with MnO2 nanosheets, the pale-yellow B-CDs@SiO2 solution took on a remarkable brown color. After added AA solution, MnO2 layer will undergo a redox reaction with AA, as TEM (Fig. 1E) and SEM (Fig. 1J) images show, the thin layer of MnO2 coated on the surface of the B-CDs@SiO2 nanocomposite will be decomposed.

In addition, the FT-IR results (Fig. 2 A) showed that B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites were successfully prepared. SiO2 exhibits the bands at 1078 cm−1, 799 cm−1 and 446 cm−1 which belong to the antisymmetric stretching vibration absorption of Si–O–Si and the band at 958 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration absorption of Si–OH. After B-CDs encapsulated in SiO2 nanosphere, B-CDs@SiO2 not only exhibited the characteristic peaks of SiO2, but provided with the band at 1667 cm−1, which corresponding to CC/CN of B-CDs. Two intense bands of MnO2 at 3421 cm−1 and 1627 cm−1 were corresponded to the H-bonded of physically adsorbed water and O–H groups, respectively. The peaks at 514 cm−1 and 454 cm−1 were assigned to the Mn–O stretching vibration of MnO2 nanosheet [34]. More particularly, it confirmed that MnO2 nanosheets has been successfully coated on B-CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites. At the same time, the XRD spectrum of the SiO2 shows the characteristic peak at 22.5°, which was attributed to the amorphous silicon (111). The XRD pattern of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites not only reveals the crystal structure of SiO2, but displays the grain structure of MnO2, in which the (311) and (440) crystalline planes of cubic MnO2 were located at 2θ = 36.9° and 65.6°, respectively (JCPDS: 42–1169), indicating a typical lamellar structure (Fig. 2B). No additional diffraction peaks were found in the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites, which indicated that the MnO2 shell had been successfully coated on B-CDs@SiO2 and could not change the crystal form of B-CDs@SiO2. The distinct optical absorption properties of the as-prepared samples were investigated by UV–vis spectra, as shown in Fig. 2C. The UV–vis spectrum of B-CDs@SiO2 displays the characteristic absorption peak of at 340 nm, which belongs to B-CDs, that B-CDs@SiO2 has been successfully prepared. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheet shows a distinct broad band centered at 380 nm, which was attributed to the d–d transition of Mn. Meanwhile, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 exhibit a broad absorption band at 400 nm. The position of the absorption peak has been red-shifted because of interaction between the two B-CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites and MnO2 nanosheet. X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Fig. 2D) shows that the red carbon spots contain five elements: C, N, O, Si and Mn.

As can be seen in Fig. S3A, the broad X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum exhibits five distinctive peaks, indicating that the R-CDs sample contains the elements Si, C, N, O, and Mn, respectively. In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. S3B), it can be decomposed into three binding energies. 284.3 eV, 285.3 eV and 288.3 eV energy peaks are caused by C–C/CC, C–N/C–OH and CO/C–O bonds, respectively [35]. The high-resolution spectra of N 1s transformed three binding energies of N (Fig. S3C), namely CN (398.9 eV), and N–N/N–C (401.6 eV). Two characteristic peaks centered at 641.8 eV and 653.1 eV were observed, the corresponding to Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 of MnO2, respectively (Fig. S3D) [31]. No additional signals were found in the XPS spectra for Mn2O3 and KMnO4 additional signals, indicating that a purer MnO2 layer was produced. The high-resolution XPS spectra at O 1s showed that O contains three binding energy states (Fig. S3E). For example, the energy at 529.1 eV originates from the Mn–O–Mn bond, the peaks of CO and C–O/Si–O bonds are located at 530.4 eV and 531.9 eV, respectively. Meanwhile, the high-resolution optical spectrum of Si 2p spectrum can be decomposed into two binding energies (Fig. S3F). The energy of 102.0 eV originates from the Si–O and Si–N bonds, respectively, while the energy peak of 102.5 originates from the C–Si–O bond.

The immunoprobe was prepared by the antibody was immobilized on the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites via electrostatic interaction. As the UV–vis absorption spectrum showed (Fig. 3 A), B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites exhibited a strong absorption peak at 400 nm. After being conjugated with Ab, the absorption peak showed a slight blue shift, and at 280 nm a new absorption band was generated. This outcome reveals that B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 composites was successfully labeled Ab. Additionally, as the FT-IR spectrum showed (Fig. 3B), Ab exhibited two strong peaks at 1627 cm−1 and 1527 cm−1, which attributed to β-sheet of the amide I band and the N–H bending with C–N stretching of amide II groups, respectively. After B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 labeled Ab, the immunoprobe not only exhibited the characteristic peaks of Ab at 1627 cm−1 and 1527 cm−1, but showed the same characteristic with B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites, indicating that Ab was labeled on the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanocomposites.

The best excitation wavelength of B-CDs located at 345 nm, and the best emission wavelength at 440 nm while the excitation wavelength and emission wavelength of B-CDs@SiO2 located at 353/430 nm (Fig. S4), respectively. This slight red shift of the excitation and emission were attributed to the stabilizing effect of SiO2 matrix on the B-CDs. Because SiO2 has no fluorescence, which can be proved that the B-CDs has encapsulated in the SiO2 nanocarrier. As seen in Fig. 4 A and Fig. S5, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 can oxidize TMB to produce oxidation of TMB (TMB+) with an obvious color change at 650 nm. After that, added H2SO4 prove H+ to terminate the catalytic reaction were produced yellow TMB2+ solution with obvious absorption at 450 nm. Meanwhile, a series of investigations revealed that MnO2 nanomaterials exhibit a broad absorption spectrum, with a large molar extinction coefficient of 9.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1, which often serve as efficient fluorescence quencher. As displayed in Fig. 4B, the board UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheet displayed a board optical absorption in visible light area, which could overlap with the fluorescence excitation and emission of the B-CDs@SiO2. This spectrum overlapping between the B-CDs@SiO2 and the MnO2 nanosheet, ensured that MnO2 nanosheet can quench the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 by IFE mechanism. Fluorescence lifetime decay curves show that MnO2 nanosheets quench the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 through IFE mechanism (Fig. 4C). Then undergo a redox reaction with small reductive biological molecules (AA) to reinstate the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2 nanocomposites (Fig. 4D). Moreover, MnO2 nanomaterials as nanozymes can take the place of natural enzymes applied in immunoassay. MnO2 nanosheet coated on B-CDs@SiO2 not only quenched the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2, but also showed the nanozyme activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-based dual-optical immunoassay
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of its excellent performances, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 as a novel signal label was employed to establish the dual-optical immunoassay for DEP detection. To achieve high assay performance, several experimental conditions for immunoassay were studied. Condition optimization content in the supporting information. Under optimal conditions (Fig. S6), the dual-optical signals were recommended for DEP based on colorimetric and fluorescence strategies. The standard curve for B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2-based dual-optical immunoassay were set up with the concentration of DEP. As in Fig. 5 A, by increasing DEP concentration, the colorimetric immunoassay with evident color change (rich yellow to light yellow). Nevertheless, a good linearity between the absorbance at 450 nm and the concentration of DEP were obtained. The linear regression equation can be expressed as A = 0.6226–0.051 × LogC (R2 = 0.996) with the rang from 0.05 ng/mL to 100 ng/mL, while the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.0034 ng/mL. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm decreased while the DEP concentration increased. Concurrently, in Fig. 5B, a linear relationship between B-CDs@SiO2 fluorescence intensity and logarithm of DEP concentration was presented as F = 9299–966 × LogC (R2 = 0.998) in the range of 0.05 ng/mL and 100 ng/mL. The detection limit was calculated to be 0.0027 ng/mL. These results strongly demonstrate that the dual-optical immunoassay provides a sensitive and reliable assignment for detecting DEP.

In order to verify the accuracy and specificity of the dual-optical immunization, the spiked plastics sample with DEP concentration of 0.5, 1.0, 2.5 ng/mL was employed to validate the accuracy of this project. The results revealed that “sense and visible” dual optical immunoassay has great accuracy and precision. The recoveries for fluorescence immunoassay ranged from 96.1% to 115.2%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 9.9% (the “sensing” part). While the colorimetric immunoassay has recoveries ranging from 98.1% to 114.9%, which has an RSD of less than 7.2% (the “visualization” part). The recovery and relative standard deviation of fluorescence immunoassay were similar to those of colorimetric immunoassay. It has been demonstrated that the sensing and visualization components established in this project are compatible, and that the dual optical immunity of “sensing and visualization” is accurate. Moreover, we also employed the GC-MS approach to validate the correctness of our created sensing and visual dual-modal immune studies by doing conventional addition recovery experiments (Table 1 ). When FIA and ELISA were used to detect the recovery of actual samples, the statistical t-test was used to compare with GC-MS/MS under the 95% confidence interval. The results showed that there was no significant difference between the developed method and the standard method GC-MS/MS, as shown in Fig.S7. The experimental results reveal that the developed “sensing and visualization” dual-optical immunoassay has a high sensitivity and can accurately detect trace diethyl phthalate in real sample.

We devised a dual-optical immunoassay approach to examine competitors such as DBP, DOP, DMP, and DCHP in order to ensure the specificity of the suggested immunosensor. We discovered that the proposed immunosensor had high specificity, indicating that competitors had no significant effect on our proposed fluorescence immunoassay, the proposed fluorescence immunoassay had excellent selectivity for DEP, as shown in Table S1. More particularly, we investigated the previous reports on the detection of DEP by immunoassay (Table 2 ). A series of investigations were performed to estimate that the dual-optical immunoassay we established has higher sensitivity than other reported tactics. The dual-optical immunoassay we established have a broad linear range and lower LOD, which exhibited the advantages of facile synthesis, simple modification and great precision compared with other reported immunoassays for DEP detection. All of these advantages indicate that our strategy has excellent potential for monitoring trace DEP in real samples.

B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 core-shell nanocomposites were successfully synthesized in this study. B-CDs encased in SiO2 nanospheres, can significantly increase the fluorescence signal and improve detection sensitivity. The biomimetic enzyme and quencher capabilities of the MnO2 nanolayer were used to provide dual-optical immunity for sensing and visualization (fluorescent and colorimetric). As immunological probes, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanomaterials not only have biomimetic enzyme activity, but their activity is also unaffected by the external environment. The sensitivity is higher than that of a standard HRP-based immunoassay. More importantly, the fluorescence of B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 immunoprobe was restored with the addition of ascorbic acid. This switch type of fluorescent probe will not be interfered with by the external medium, which can improve the sensitivity. In addition, dual-optical immunoassay results can be corroborated with each other to ensure the accuracy of the test results. In this study, the proposed dual-optical method showed acceptable accuracy and precision for the detection of DEP in food-contacted plastics. In a word, B-CDs@SiO2@MnO2 nanomaterials as fluorescence signal amplification and color reaction tags have a lot of potential for monitoring trace DEP exposure to humans from food-contacted plastics.

Biru Chen: Writing-original draft, Methodology, Date curation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Lei Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software. Qianqian Yang: Investigation. Mingcui Zhang: Supervision, Writing-review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175004), China; the Anhui provincial Natural Science Foundation (2108085MB68), China; and the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (GXXT-2020-074), China.
